response_text:
# JavaScript's Threading Model

## The Core Reality

**JavaScript is single-threaded.** It does not have a built-in multi-threading model in the traditional sense. There is one main thread, one call stack, and one piece of code executing at any given moment.

```javascript
// This all runs on ONE thread, sequentially
console.log("first");
console.log("second");  // "second" cannot run until "first" finishes
console.log("third");
```

---

## How JavaScript Handles Concurrency

JavaScript achieves *concurrency* (not parallelism) through the **Event Loop**.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Call Stack                        │
│  [ main() → setTimeout cb → fetch cb → ... ]        │
└──────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────┘
                       │
┌──────────────────────▼──────────────────────────────┐
│                   Event Loop                         │
│   Continuously checks: "Is the call stack empty?"   │
│   If yes → pull next task from queue                 │
└──────────┬────────────────────────┬─────────────────┘
           │                        │
┌──────────▼──────────┐  ┌──────────▼──────────────── ┐
│   Macrotask Queue   │  │     Microtask Queue         │
│  setTimeout         │  │  Promise.then()             │
│  setInterval        │  │  queueMicrotask()           │
│  I/O callbacks      │  │  MutationObserver           │
│  UI events          │  │                             │
└─────────────────────┘  └─────────────────────────────┘
```

### Execution Priority

```javascript
console.log("1 - synchronous");

setTimeout(() => console.log("2 - macrotask"), 0);

Promise.resolve()
  .then(() => console.log("3 - microtask"))
  .then(() => console.log("4 - microtask
